    "R1.2": "The methodology chain (CTC training → WFST-constrained beam search → lexicon FST) must logically support the headline claim that 'the model learns when to invoke external tools'; checking premise-to-conclusion coherence is a natural load under a general review.",
    "R1.3": "Specific WER figures (2.8%, 5.9% rare-word, 9.7% medical) are the primary evidence; verifying that these figures align with the experimental setup described and that the claimed gains are properly scoped is central to any credible opinion on the paper.",

  "clarification_question": "Is your primary interest (a) the technical soundness and evidence quality of the methodology and results, (b) the novelty of the approach relative to prior tool-augmented or lexicon-constrained ASR work, or (c) the writing quality and structural presentation of the paper?"

rationale.R1.1: Delegation 'What do you think?' applied to a research artifact naturally invites evaluation of the thesis (tool-augmented ASR), the gap statement (OOV/rare-noun weakness), and the contribution claim (self-supervised tool invocation); reconstructing whether these cohere is central to any plausible reading.
rationale.R1.2: The methodology chain (CTC training → WFST-constrained beam search → lexicon FST) must logically support the headline claim that 'the model learns when to invoke external tools'; checking premise-to-conclusion coherence is a natural load under a general review.
rationale.R1.3: Specific WER figures (2.8%, 5.9% rare-word, 9.7% medical) are the primary evidence; verifying that these figures align with the experimental setup described and that the claimed gains are properly scoped is central to any credible opinion on the paper.
rationale.R1.4: A research paper review almost always implicates novelty: the ToolASR framing borrows from LLM tool-use literature and applies WFST constraints that predate neural ASR; assessing the genuine delta versus nearest prior work is material to forming a useful opinion.

parsed.clarification_question: Is your primary interest (a) the technical soundness and evidence quality of the methodology and results, (b) the novelty of the approach relative to prior tool-augmented or lexicon-constrained ASR work, or (c) the writing quality and structural presentation of the paper?
